text.title: Exploratory Testing Charters Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the Exploratory Testing Charters methodology. Each pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale; the last rule binds the decision tree skip path.

rule.statement: Charter MUST start with a one-sentence mission of the form 'Explore X with Y to discover Z'. Verbose multi-paragraph missions are rejected.
rule.rationale: one-sentence mission is the cognitive anchor.

rule.statement: Charter declares a timebox (60–120 min). Open-ended sessions are rejected.
rule.rationale: Time-boxing forces prioritisation inside the session.

rule.statement: Charter lists ≥3 threat hypotheses ('what could go wrong here'). Empty threat list defaults to wandering.
rule.rationale: Threats give the tester something to actively disprove.

rule.statement: Debrief MUST capture: time spent on session vs setup vs investigation, charter coverage (full/partial), issues found (with severity), notes, and one follow-up action. Free-form debriefs are rejected.
rule.rationale: Debrief comparability across sessions feeds metric trends.

rule.statement: Every issue captured in the debrief MUST carry a one-line repro AND severity. Issues without repro convert to 'open question'.
rule.rationale: unrepro'able issues hide in backlogs forever.

rule.statement: Charter has a single named author; debrief has a single named tester; they may be the same person.
rule.rationale: Ownership clarifies accountability for next-step actions.

rule.statement: When the decision tree routes to `skip-this-methodology`, the agent MUST emit a structured `skip` decision (with reason + suggested-sibling) rather than running Exploratory Testing Charters under degraded conditions.
rule.rationale: decision-tree contract. The decision tree is the only place that knows the routing; skipping must be observable so the orchestrator can log the routed-elsewhere path.
